mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes‑Benz SL600 / SL65 AMG V12 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned SL600 / SL65 AMG V12 engine typically costs £5,000–£9,500 supply only, with the M275 6.0L AMG variant at the higher end. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,800 depending on the specialist. A full engine replacement on a Mercedes V12 can range from £9,000 to £13,000. Rebuilt engines are available from specialist suppliers with 12‑month warranties. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your SL.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes‑Benz SL600 / SL65 AMG V12 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which engine era your SL has. The M120 (R129, 1992‑1997) is widely considered bulletproof mechanically, making it the most reliable of the three V12 generations. The M137 (R230, 1999‑2001) was short‑lived and has a reputation for being notoriously unreliable — best avoided. The M275 (R230, 2003‑2012) delivers breathtaking twin‑turbo performance but demands careful ownership — common issues include coil pack failure, oil leaks, and timing chain stretch.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes‑Benz SL600 / SL65 AMG V12?

acceptedAnswer.text: M120 (R129 1992‑1997): Biodegradable wiring harness (1992‑1995), coil pack failures, valve cover oil leaks. M137 (R230 1999‑2001): Notoriously unreliable — cylinder wall wear, electronic issues. M275 (R230 2003‑2012): Coil pack failure (two packs, ~£1,000 each — often caused by oil from leaking valve cover gaskets), oil leaks from valve covers and turbo seals, timing chain stretch, and transmission issues. The SL65 AMG produces 603 hp and 738 lb‑ft torque.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an SL600 / SL65 AMG V12 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the vehicle value and condition. Against a well‑kept R230 SL65 AMG worth £40,000–£60,000+, a £8,000–£10,000 recon is 15–20% of the car's value — often worth it. Against a tired R129 with significant body or ABC suspension issues, the same recon may approach our 70% Mercedes‑Benz threshold — get a scrap valuation first. The M120 R129 is a future classic and worth preserving.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes‑Benz SL600 / SL65 AMG V12 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: R129 (1992‑1997): M120.981. R230 (1999‑2001): M137.970. R230 (2003‑2006): M275.950 (SL600 5.5L). R230 (2004‑2012): M275.981 (SL65 AMG 6.0L). Check the VIN plate or vehicle documentation. The engine code is also stamped on the engine block.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the M275 coil packs, or do they need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The coil packs must be replaced — they are a wear item on the M275 due to heat exposure and oil contamination from leaking valve cover gaskets. Each coil pack costs approximately £1,000, and replacing both as a set is recommended when one fails. Ignoring a misfire can damage the catalytic converters — an even more expensive repair. At the first sign of misfires or rough running, book an inspection immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes‑Benz SL600 / SL65 AMG V12 Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect current SL600 / SL65 AMG replacement costs. The M275 AMG 6.0L variant typically sits at the higher end of the range. See the Era Map above for generation‑specific pricing.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an SL600 / SL65 AMG engine typically takes 14–18 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). Engine bay access is notoriously tight on the R230, increasing labour times.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: SL600 / SL65 AMG V12 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Replace both packs as a set — prevent further issues

rows.problem: Oil leaks (valve cover, turbo seals — M275)
rows.whenItMakesSense: Catch it early — before coil packs and turbos are damaged
Essential maintenance — catch before catastrophic failure
Piston‑valve contact means little is left to repair
Not economically repairable — avoid M137 if possible

buyingChecks.title: Buying This SL600 / SL65 AMG V12? Check These First
buyIf: * R129 (1992‑1997) with M120 — bulletproof mechanically, wiring harness replaced
* R230 (2003‑2012) with M275 and recent coil pack replacement (both banks)
* Full Mercedes service history with regular oil changes (critical for V12 longevity)
* No misfires or rough running (coil pack health)
* No oil leaks around valve covers or turbos (seal health)
* Post‑2003 with the twin‑turbo M275 — the most powerful and refined SL V12
avoidIf: * R230 (1999‑2001) with M137 — notoriously unreliable, cylinder wall wear issues
* M275 with misfires and no coil pack history — budget for coil pack replacement immediately
* M275 with oil leaks — valve cover gaskets or turbo seals may be failing
* Cold‑start rattling and no timing chain history — budget for chain work
* No service history — V12s are unforgiving of neglect
* Any history of engine failure or major repairs without documentation

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The SL600 and SL65 AMG V12 represent the pinnacle of Mercedes‑Benz open‑top motoring — a handcrafted twelve‑cylinder experience that defines the brand's flagship roadster. The M120 (R129, 1992‑1997) is widely considered bulletproof mechanically, making it the most reliable of the three V12 generations — a genuine future classic. The M137 (R230, 1999‑2001) was short‑lived and has a reputation for being notoriously unreliable — best avoided. The M275 (R230, 2003‑2012) delivers breathtaking twin‑turbo performance — 493‑603 hp and up to 738 lb‑ft torque — but demands careful ownership. Common issues include coil pack failure (£1,000 per pack), oil leaks from valve cover gaskets and turbo seals, and timing chain stretch. The M275 AMG 6.0‑litre in the SL65 is one of Affalterbach's most legendary creations. With typical SL values ranging from £15,000 (R129) to £50,000+ (R230 SL65), an engine replacement is a major financial commitment — a well‑maintained M120 R129 or a post‑2003 M275 SL65 with documented coil pack and maintenance history is the safest choice. The M137 is best avoided entirely.

cta.label: → Compare SL600 / SL65 AMG V12 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes‑Benz SL600 / SL65 AMG V12 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Coil Packs — Check for smooth idle; any misfires may indicate coil pack failure (M275) — see the map above
* Cold Start — Listen for rattling or clattering (timing chain warning — M275)
* Oil Leaks — Check around valve covers and turbo areas for leaks (M275)
* Wiring Harness — For R129 (1992‑1995): check if biodegradable wiring harness has been replaced (M120)
* Service History — Including which engine era (M120, M137 or M275) and oil‑change discipline
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes read; unresolved issues may indicate coil, timing, oil or sensor problems
* Transmission — Check 5G‑Tronic operation; early M275 produced too much torque for the gearbox
* ABC Suspension — R230 ABC suspension is a major maintenance item — check for leaks and function
* MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures
* Roof Operation — R230 folding hardtop mechanism is complex and expensive to repair

items.question: Is the Mercedes‑Benz SL600 / SL65 AMG V12 engine reliable?
items.answer: It depends on which engine era your SL has. The M120 (R129, 1992‑1997) is widely considered bulletproof mechanically, making it the most reliable of the three V12 generations. The M137 (R230, 1999‑2001) was short‑lived and has a reputation for being notoriously unreliable — best avoided. The M275 (R230, 2003‑2012) delivers breathtaking twin‑turbo performance but demands careful ownership — common issues include coil pack failure, oil leaks, and timing chain stretch.

items.question: What are the common problems with the Mercedes‑Benz SL600 / SL65 AMG V12?
items.answer: M120 (R129 1992‑1997): Biodegradable wiring harness (1992‑1995), coil pack failures, valve cover oil leaks. M137 (R230 1999‑2001): Notoriously unreliable — cylinder wall wear, electronic issues. M275 (R230 2003‑2012): Coil pack failure (two packs, ~£1,000 each — often caused by oil from leaking valve cover gaskets), oil leaks from valve covers and turbo seals, timing chain stretch, and transmission issues (5G‑Tronic not always able to handle the torque). The SL65 AMG produces 603 hp and 738 lb‑ft torque.

items.question: Is it worth replacing an SL600 / SL65 AMG V12 engine, or should I scrap the car?
items.answer: Depends on the vehicle value and condition. Against a well‑kept R230 SL65 AMG worth £40,000–£60,000+, a £8,000–£10,000 recon is 15–20% of the car's value — often worth it. Against a tired R129 with significant body or ABC suspension issues, the same recon may approach our 70% Mercedes‑Benz threshold — get a scrap valuation first. items.question: How do I find my Mercedes‑Benz SL600 / SL65 AMG V12 engine code?
items.answer: R129 (1992‑1997): M120.981. R230 (1999‑2001): M137.970. R230 (2003‑2006): M275.950 (SL600 5.5L). R230 (2004‑2012): M275.981 (SL65 AMG 6.0L). Check the VIN plate or vehicle documentation. The engine code is also stamped on the engine block. The SL600 produces 389‑493 hp depending on era; the SL65 AMG produces 603 hp.

items.question: Can I repair the M275 coil packs, or do they need replacement?
items.answer: The coil packs must be replaced — they are a wear item on the M275 due to heat exposure and oil contamination from leaking valve cover gaskets. Each coil pack costs approximately £1,000, and replacing both as a set is recommended when one fails. Ignoring a misfire can damage the catalytic converters — an even more expensive repair. At the first sign of misfires or rough running, book an inspection immediately. Valve cover gaskets should also be replaced to prevent oil contamination of the new coil packs.
